***Reasonable Doubt*** is the debut studio album by American rapper [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z"). It was released on June 25, 1996, by his own record label [Roc-A-Fella Records](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records") and distributed by [Priority Records](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records"). The album features production provided by [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier"), [Ski](/wiki/Ski_(producer) "Ski (producer)"), [Knobody](/wiki/Knobody "Knobody") and [Clark Kent](/wiki/Clark_Kent_(producer) "Clark Kent (producer)"), and also includes [guest appearances](/wiki/Guest_appearance "Guest appearance") from [Memphis Bleek](/wiki/Memphis_Bleek "Memphis Bleek"), [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige"), [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O"), and [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G."), among others. The album features [mafioso rap](/wiki/Mafioso_rap "Mafioso rap") themes and gritty lyrics about the "hustler" lifestyle and material obsessions.

*Reasonable Doubt* debuted at number 23 on the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), on which it charted for 18 weeks. It was promoted with four singles; including "[Ain't No Nigga](/wiki/Ain%27t_No_Nigga "Ain't No Nigga")" and "[Can't Knock the Hustle](/wiki/Can%27t_Knock_the_Hustle_(Jay-Z_song) "Can't Knock the Hustle (Jay-Z song)")". *Reasonable Doubt* was certified [Platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA) and,[[2]](#cite_note-riaa-2) as of 2006, has sold 1.5 million copies in the United States.[[3]](#cite_note-3) A critical success, it has been ranked on several publications' lists of hip-hop's greatest albums, while many hip hop fans have viewed it as Jay-Z's best work. In August 2019, *Reasonable Doubt* was released to digital and streaming platforms under [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation")'s independent label, Equity Distribution.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

In 1989, aspiring rapper Jay-Z was recruited by mentor Jaz-O to appear on his song "Hawaiian Sophie".[[5]](#cite_note-VH1-5) He appeared on two more Jaz-O songs in the next year, but after Jaz-O was dropped from his record label, Jay-Z dealt drugs to support himself.[[5]](#cite_note-VH1-5) He continued to pursue a rap career and appeared on two songs from [Original Flavor](/wiki/Original_Flavor "Original Flavor")'s 1993 album *[Beyond Flavor](/wiki/Beyond_Flavor "Beyond Flavor")*. Jay-Z then caught [Big Daddy Kane](/wiki/Big_Daddy_Kane "Big Daddy Kane")'s attention and toured with him; they collaborated on Kane's 1994 [posse cut](/wiki/Posse_cut "Posse cut") "Show & Prove" along with [Wu-Tang Clan](/wiki/Wu-Tang_Clan "Wu-Tang Clan")'s [Ol' Dirty Bastard](/wiki/Ol%27_Dirty_Bastard "Ol' Dirty Bastard"), Wu-Tang affiliate [Shyheim](/wiki/Shyheim "Shyheim"), [Sauce Money](/wiki/Sauce_Money "Sauce Money"), and Scoob Lover.[[5]](#cite_note-VH1-5)

Despite the exposure he received from Kane, Jay-Z was still without a record deal. He began selling tapes from his car with help from friend [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash").[[6]](#cite_note-Yahoo-6) The success of his street-level marketing led to a deal with [Payday Records](/wiki/Payday_Records "Payday Records"), which released his first solo single, "[In My Lifetime](/wiki/In_My_Lifetime_(song) "In My Lifetime (song)")" and its [B-side](/wiki/B-side "B-side") "I Can't Get wit Dat". In an unconventional move, Jay-Z then spurned the record contract he had long sought and left Payday Records to form his own label, [Roc-A-Fella Records](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records"), with Damon Dash and Kareem "Biggs" Burke. Jay-Z later explained that he thought he could do a better job of marketing his records on his own:

> [Payday] eventually signed me to a deal, but were acting shady the whole time, like they didn't know how to work a record or something. The things that they were setting up for me I could have done myself. They had me traveling places to do instores, and my product wasn't even available in the store. We shot one video, but when the time came for me to do the video for the second single, I had to be cut out. They gave me the money and I started my own company. There was a little arguing back and forth, but our conflict finally got resolved. The bottom line was they wasn't doing their job, so I had to get out of there.[[6]](#cite_note-Yahoo-6)

Jay-Z rented a small, cheap office for Roc-A-Fella Records on John Street in one of the "dreariest parts of the busiest city in the world".[[6]](#cite_note-Yahoo-6) Jay-Z and his compatriots thought of their low-rent headquarters as a "starting point" that would eventually lead them to Manhattan.[[6]](#cite_note-Yahoo-6) In 1995 and early 1996, Jay-Z appeared on records by [Big L](/wiki/Big_L "Big L") and [Mic Geronimo](/wiki/Mic_Geronimo "Mic Geronimo"), further raising his profile. At this point, he was still considered an "[underground](/wiki/Underground_hip_hop "Underground hip hop")"[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) rapper with a "new jack" style.[[8]](#cite_note-raprev-8)

## Recording

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Recording")]

*Reasonable Doubt* was recorded at [D&D Studios](/wiki/D%26D_Studios "D&D Studios") and [mixed](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)") at Platinum Island, however, its beats were formed elsewhere. "[Can't Knock the Hustle](/wiki/Can%27t_Knock_the_Hustle_(Jay-Z_song) "Can't Knock the Hustle (Jay-Z song)")" was produced by [Knobody](/wiki/Knobody "Knobody") at his mother's home in 1994, while the vocals were recorded on tour at a studio in Tampa, Florida named Progressive Music with Mary J. Blige. [Ski](/wiki/Ski_(producer) "Ski (producer)") produced "[Feelin' It](/wiki/Feelin%27_It_(Jay-Z_song) "Feelin' It (Jay-Z song)")" and "Politics as Usual" while recording with [Camp Lo](/wiki/Camp_Lo "Camp Lo").[[9]](#cite_note-xxl-9) The recording sessions were often competitive; Ski and [Clark Kent](/wiki/Clark_Kent_(producer) "Clark Kent (producer)") created similar beats for "Politics as Usual", but Ski submitted his to Jay-Z first causing his to appear on the album.[[9]](#cite_note-xxl-9) "Brooklyn's Finest" was a competitive, though friendly battle between Jay-Z and [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") in which Jay-Z tried proving that he is of Biggie's caliber, while Biggie tried brushing his rhymes off as insignificant.[[9]](#cite_note-xxl-9) Although the rappers had already met on the set for the "[Dead Presidents](/wiki/Dead_Presidents_(song) "Dead Presidents (song)")" music video, they discovered that neither wrote down their rhymes while recording.[[9]](#cite_note-xxl-9) The recording of "Brooklyn's Finest" spanned two months and moved from D&D Studios to Giant Studios where the Clark Kent-sung chorus was recorded.[[9]](#cite_note-xxl-9) Irv Gotti and Dame Dash noted that Nas was supposed to be featured on "Bring It On" and "Can I Live" but sessions for neither song ended up happening.[[10]](#cite_note-l061-10)

## Music and lyrics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Music and lyrics")]

An [East Coast hip-hop](/wiki/East_Coast_hip-hop "East Coast hip-hop") record,[[11]](#cite_note-11) *Reasonable Doubt* was noted for having [mafioso rap](/wiki/Mafioso_rap "Mafioso rap") themes,[[12]](#cite_note-theSource-12)[[13]](#cite_note-XXLnumbers-13) with lyrics characterized by *[Stylus Magazine](/wiki/Stylus_Magazine "Stylus Magazine")* as "gritty [realism](/wiki/Literary_realism "Literary realism")".[[14]](#cite_note-stylus-14) Writer [dream hampton](/wiki/Dream_Hampton "Dream Hampton") believed that although rappers had alluded to hustling before, Jay-Z "talks about what it can do to a person's inner peace, and what it can do to their mind".[[5]](#cite_note-VH1-5) Jay-Z later said, "the studio was like a [psychiatrist](/wiki/Psychiatrist "Psychiatrist")'s couch for me" while recording *Reasonable Doubt*.[[15]](#cite_note-RS500-15) [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic")'s Steve Huey described him as "a street hustler from the projects who rapped about what he knew—and he was very, very good at it...detailing his experiences on the streets with disarming honesty".[[16]](#cite_note-AMG-16) Huey summarizes the album's subject matter saying:

> He's cocky bordering on arrogant, but playful and witty, and exudes an effortless, unaffected cool throughout. And even if he's rapping about rising to the top instead of being there, his material obsessions are already apparent [...] the album's defining cut might [...] be the brief "22 Two's," which not only demonstrates Jay-Z's extraordinary talent as a pure freestyle rapper, but also preaches a subtle message through its club hostess: Bad behavior gets in the way of making money. Perhaps that's why Jay-Z waxes reflective, not enthusiastic, about the darker side of the streets.[[16]](#cite_note-AMG-16)

AllMusic's Jason Birchmeier writes that the album's production exhibits characteristics of "the pre-[gangsta](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") era, a foregone era when samples fueled the beats and [turntablism](/wiki/Turntablism "Turntablism") supplied the hooks", which "sets *Reasonable Doubt* apart from Jay-Z's later work".[[17]](#cite_note-AMG2-17) "Can't Knock the Hustle" features a smooth beat.[[18]](#cite_note-ign-18) "Politics as Usual" has an [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") sound and a sample of "Hurry Up This Way Again" by [the Stylistics](/wiki/The_Stylistics "The Stylistics").[[18]](#cite_note-ign-18) "Dead Presidents" samples [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas")' voice from "[The World Is Yours](/wiki/The_World_Is_Yours_(Nas_song) "The World Is Yours (Nas song)")" in its chorus.[[19]](#cite_note-skiinterview-19) According to [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN")'s Spence D., "Ski brings back the stripped down piano fill style lending the track a late night jazz vibe" on "Feelin' It", and "22 Two's" has a "mournful jazz inclined groove" that prominently features [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument").[[18]](#cite_note-ign-18) "Coming of Age" contains a Clark Kent-produced beat that samples the melody and drums from "Inside You" by [Eddie Henderson](/wiki/Eddie_Henderson_(musician) "Eddie Henderson (musician)").[[18]](#cite_note-ign-18)

*Reasonable Doubt* was met with widespread acclaim from music critics.[[30]](#cite_note-village1-30) Charlie Braxton of *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)")* praised Jay-Z for evolving "from hip-hop [sidekick](/wiki/Hype_man "Hype man") to Mafia-style front man, blowing up the spot with vivid tales about the economic reality fueling what's left of contemporary ghetto politics".[[27]](#cite_note-thesource-27) *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*'s Dimitri Ehrlich commended him for rapping "with an irresistible confidence, a voice that exudes tough-guy authenticity", also noting the "unadorned but suitably militant" production.[[21]](#cite_note-Ehrlich-21) Tonya Pendleton of the *[Los Angeles Daily News](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Daily_News "Los Angeles Daily News")* stated that the album "hits you with rap's trends – [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige") riffs, [Foxy Brown](/wiki/Foxy_Brown_(rapper) "Foxy Brown (rapper)") rhymes, [Isley Brothers](/wiki/The_Isley_Brothers "The Isley Brothers") loops and more fashion info than [Cindy Crawford](/wiki/Cindy_Crawford "Cindy Crawford")", adding that "his sassy way with a lyric transcends the material" on the album.[[23]](#cite_note-Pendleton-23)

### Retrospect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Retrospect")]

*Reasonable Doubt* has often been considered by many fans to be Jay-Z's best record.[[31]](#cite_note-AMGBio-31) According to Birchmeier, it differed from his subsequent albums by lacking "[pop-crossover](/wiki/Crossover_(music) "Crossover (music)")" songs and hits.[[31]](#cite_note-AMGBio-31) Shaheem Reid of [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") explained, "*Reasonable Doubt* might not have the radio hits or club bangers of many of his other albums, but it may be Jay at his most lyrical—and certainly at his most honest, according to him".[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) Huey said the lyrical appeal lied within Jay-Z's "effortless, unaffected cool" flow, and knack for "writing some of the most acrobatic rhymes heard in quite some time". According to Huey, this "helped *Reasonable Doubt* rank as one of the finest albums of [New York's hip-hop renaissance](/wiki/East_Coast_hip_hop#Renaissance_(1990s) "East Coast hip hop") of the '90s".[[16]](#cite_note-AMG-16) Birchmeier, on the other hand, believed the superior quality of producers was more responsible for the album's reputation as a classic more so than Jay-Z.[[17]](#cite_note-AMG2-17) In a retrospective review for *[MSN Music](/wiki/MSN_Music "MSN Music")*, [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") said the album was "designed for the hip-hop [cognoscenti](/wiki/Expert "Expert") and street aesthetes who still swear he never topped it," finding it "richer than any outsider could have known, and benefiting from everything we've since learned about the minor crack baron who put his money where his mouth was. You can hear him marshalling a discipline known to few rappers and many crack barons, and that asceticism undercuts the intrinsic delight of his rhymes".[[24]](#cite_note-Christgau-24)

## Commercial performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Commercial performance")]

*Reasonable Doubt* was released by Roc-A-Fella on June 25, 1996, through a distribution deal with [Priority](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records").[[13]](#cite_note-XXLnumbers-13) It was not an immediate success, reaching a peak position of 23 on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart, with a sales total of 43,000 in its first week.[[32]](#cite_note-Stopthebreaks.com-32) It spent 18 weeks on the chart, and 55 weeks on the [Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums](/wiki/Top_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Albums "Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums"), on which it reached number 3.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The album was promoted with the release of four singles, none of which reached the [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40"); "Ain't No Nigga" was the highest-charting single at number 50, "Can't Knock the Hustle" and "Feelin' It" did not peak higher than 70, and "Dead Presidents" did not chart altogether.[[34]](#cite_note-complex-34) By year end, the album had sold 420,000 copies.[[13]](#cite_note-XXLnumbers-13)

On February 7, 2002, *Reasonable Doubt* was certified [Platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA), for shipments of a million copies in the US.[[2]](#cite_note-riaa-2) It remains the lowest charting album of Jay-Z's career.[[35]](#cite_note-AMGchartalbum-35) According to *[Respect](/wiki/Respect._(magazine) "Respect. (magazine)")* magazine, it had sold 1.5 million copies in the United States by 2006.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Legacy and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reasonable_Doubt_(album)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Legacy and influence")]

Since its initial reception, *Reasonable Doubt* has received further acclaim from music critics and writers.[[34]](#cite_note-complex-34) According to *[Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")*'s Ryan Schreiber, it has often been "considered one of hip-hop's landmark albums",[[37]](#cite_note-pfork-37) while Birchmeier said it was viewed like Nas' *[Illmatic](/wiki/Illmatic "Illmatic")* (1994) as a classic hip hop album by a young rapper about their street and criminal experiences.[[16]](#cite_note-AMG-16) *Reasonable Doubt* helped transfigure [gangsta rap](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") into [mafioso rap](/wiki/Mafioso_rap "Mafioso rap"), popularizing the subgenre and the imagery of high class, expensive lifestyles and tastes in hip hop, including drinking [Cristal](/wiki/Cristal_(champagne) "Cristal (champagne)"), driving [Lexus](/wiki/Lexus "Lexus") automobiles, and living out the plots of films such as *[Scarface](/wiki/Scarface_(1983_film) "Scarface (1983 film)")* and *[Carlito's Way](/wiki/Carlito%27s_Way "Carlito's Way")*.[[38]](#cite_note-clarion-38) In the opinion of [Miles Marshall Lewis](/wiki/Miles_Marshall_Lewis "Miles Marshall Lewis"), *Reasonable Doubt* was a "seminal" work that "shocked the world ... a personal touchstone for fans then Jay's own age who were getting their own hustles on—hip hop's young, gifted, and black".[[30]](#cite_note-village1-30) Jay-Z said that recreating *Reasonable Doubt* would be challenging, as he was living a different lifestyle with a completely different state of mind when he wrote the album.[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7)[[19]](#cite_note-skiinterview-19)[[37]](#cite_note-pfork-37)

*Reasonable Doubt* was named one of the greatest hip hop albums of all time by *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)")* in 1998,[[39]](#cite_note-source-39) *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")*, who ranked it seventh on their 2002 list,[[40]](#cite_note-40) [MTV.com](/wiki/MTV.com "MTV.com"), who ranked it sixth on their 2005 list,[[41]](#cite_note-41) and [About.com](/wiki/About.com "About.com")'s Henry Adaso; Adaso ranked it as the 14th greatest hip hop album,[[42]](#cite_note-42) the second best rap record of 1996,[[43]](#cite_note-43) and the fifth most "essential" hip hop album ever.[[44]](#cite_note-44) *[Blender](/wiki/Blender_(magazine) "Blender (magazine)")* included *Reasonable Doubt* on the magazine's 2003 list of "500 CDs You Must Own Before You Die".[[45]](#cite_note-45) That same year, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* ranked it number 248 on their list of the [500 Greatest Albums of All Time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_500_Greatest_Albums_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums of All Time"),[[15]](#cite_note-RS500-15) number 250 on the 2012 revision, and the album's rank shot up to number 67 on the 2020 reboot of the list.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) The magazine also named it the 17th best album of the 1990s.[[48]](#cite_note-48) It was included in *Vibe*'s "51 Essential Albums" (2004),[[49]](#cite_note-49) and *[Hip Hop Connection](/wiki/Hip_Hop_Connection "Hip Hop Connection")*'s "The 100 Greatest Rap Albums 1995–2005".[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In 2006, Jay-Z performed the songs from *Reasonable Doubt* at the [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") to celebrate its tenth anniversary. The concert's band included [the Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots")' drummer [Questlove](/wiki/Questlove "Questlove"), the Illadelphonics, a 50-piece orchestra dubbed the Hustla's Symphony and [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"), the performance's [disc jockey](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey").[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) On "Can't Knock the Hustle", [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") replaced Mary J. Blige, who was preparing for her [Breakthrough Tour](/wiki/The_Breakthrough "The Breakthrough") at the time.[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) Jay-Z rapped the Notorious B.I.G.'s verses on "Brooklyn's Finest", and Jaz-O's verse was left out of "Bring It On".[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) Jay-Z added a verse to "22 Two's" in which he says variations of the words "for/four" 44 times over the beat of "[Can I Kick It?](/wiki/Can_I_Kick_It%3F "Can I Kick It?")" by [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest").[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) Other alterations include Jay-Z changing a lyrical mention of [Cristal](/wiki/Cristal_(champagne) "Cristal (champagne)") to [Dom Pérignon](/wiki/Dom_P%C3%A9rignon_(wine) "Dom Pérignon (wine)") and Jay-Z's band "spruc[ing] up tracks like 'Regrets' to add more energy".[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) Celebrities such as [Alicia Keys](/wiki/Alicia_Keys "Alicia Keys"), [Young Jeezy](/wiki/Young_Jeezy "Young Jeezy"), [Jadakiss](/wiki/Jadakiss "Jadakiss"), [Chris Tucker](/wiki/Chris_Tucker "Chris Tucker"), [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James") and [Carmelo Anthony](/wiki/Carmelo_Anthony "Carmelo Anthony") attended the concert.[[7]](#cite_note-VH1News-7) 3,000 tickets were put on sale; all were sold within two minutes according to Roc-A-Fella Records' website.[[51]](#cite_note-pitchforkmedia-51)
